concepts.title: Terms Related to hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Terms Related to Hyperbola 

Symmetry : Since only even powers of $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ occur in the above equation, so the curve is symmetrical about both the axes.

Foci : $\text{S}$ and $\text{{S}'}$ are the two foci of the hyperbola and their coordinates are (ae, 0$)$ and $(-$ ae, 0$)$ respectively, then distance between foci is given by $\text{S}{{\text{S}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=2\text{ae}$.

Directries : ZM and $\text{{Z}'{M}'}$ are the two directrices of the hyperbola and their equations are $\text{x}=\frac{\text{a}}{\text{e}}$and $\text{x}=-\frac{\text{a}}{\text{e}}$ respectively, then the distance between directries is given by $\text{Z}{{\text{Z}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{2\text{a}}{\text{e}}$

Axes : The lines $\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ and $\text{B}{{\text{B}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ are called the transverse axis and conjugate axis respectively of the hyperbola. The length of transverse axis $=\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=2\text{a}$. The length of conjugate axis $=\text{B}{{\text{B}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}$.

Centre : The point of intersection $\text{C}$ of the axes of hyperbola is called the center of the hyperbola. All chords passing through $\text{C}$, are bisected at $\text{C}$.

Vertices : The point $A\equiv \left( a,0 \right)$ and ${A}'\equiv \left( -a,0 \right)$ where the curve meets the line joining the foci $\text{S}$ and $\text{{S}'}$ are called the vertices of the hyperbola.

Focal chord : A chord of the hyperbola passing through its focus is called a focal chord.

Focal distance : The focal distance of any point $\left( x,y \right)$ on the hyperbola $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ are

ex $-\text{a}$ and $\text{ex}+\text{a}$.

The difference of the focal distance of any point on the hyperbola is constant and equal to the length of the transverse axis of the hyperbola. If $\text{P}$ is any hyperbola,

then $\text{{S}'P}-\text{SP}=2\text{a}=$ Transverse axis.

Latus Rectum : If $\text{L}{{\text{L}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{N}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$ are the latus rectum of the hyperbola then these lines are perpendicular to the transverse axis $\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$, passing through the foci $\text{S}$ and $\text{{S}'}$ respectively.

$\text{L}\equiv \left( \text{ae},\frac{{{\text{b}}^{2}}}{\text{a}} \right),\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {L}'}\equiv \left( \text{ae},-\frac{{{\text{b}}^{2}}}{\text{a}} \right),\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}\equiv \left( -\text{ae},\frac{{{\text{b}}^{2}}}{\text{a}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {N}'}\equiv \left( -\text{ae},\frac{-{{\text{b}}^{2}}}{\text{a}} \right)$

Length of latus rectum $={L}'=\frac{2{{b}^{2}}}{a}={N}'$

Eccentricity of the Hyperbola 

We know that $\text{SP}=\text{ePM}$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ S}{{\text{P}}^{2}}={{\text{e}}^{2}}\text{P}{{\text{M}}^{2}}$ or $\mathrm{SP}^2=\mathrm{e}^2 \mathrm{PM}^2$

$\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)}=1$

On comparing with $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$, we get ${{b}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)$ or $e=\sqrt{1+\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}}$

or $\quad e=\sqrt{1+\frac{{{(\text{ conjugate axis })}^{2}}}{{{(\text{ transverse axis })}^{2}}}}$

concepts.title: General equation of hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
General Equation of Hyperbola 

The equation of hyperbola, whose focus is point $\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$ directrix is $\text{lx}+\text{my}+\text{n}=0$ & eccentricity 'e' is given by ${{(x-h)}^{2}}+{{(y-k)}^{2}}=\frac{{{e}^{2}}{{(lx+my+n)}^{2}}}{\left( {{l}^{2}}+{{m}^{2}} \right)}$

Illustration 1 

Find the equation of the hyperbola whose focus is $\left( 1,2 \right)$ directrix is the line $x+y+1=0$ and eccentricity $\frac{3}{2}$. 

Solution: 

Let $\text{S}\left( 1,2 \right)$ be the focus and let $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ be a point on the hyperbola. Draw perpendicular PM from $\text{P}$ on the directrix $\text{x}+\text{y}+1=0$. Then $\text{SP}=\text{e PM}$

$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(\mathrm{x}-1)^2+(\mathrm{y}-2)^2} \quad=\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2}}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)+(y-2)^2=\frac{9}{4}\left[\frac{(x+y+1)^2}{2}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \quad 8\left[(x-1)^2+(y-2)^2\right]=9\left[(x+y+1)^2\right]$

$\Rightarrow \quad 8 x^2+8 y^2-16 x-32 y+40$ $=9 x^2+9 y^2+9+18 x y+18 x+18 y$

$\Rightarrow \quad x^2+y^2+18 x y+34 x+50 y-31=0$

This is the equation of the required hyperbola.

Illustration 2 

Find the eccentricity of the conic represented by ${{x}^{2}}-{{y}^{2}}-4x+4y+16=0$ 

Solution: 

We have ${{\text{x}}^{2}}-{{\text{y}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+4\text{y}+16=0$ 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( {{x}^{2}}-4x \right)-\left( {{y}^{2}}-4y \right)=-16 & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( {{x}^{2}}-4x+4 \right)-\left( {{y}^{2}}-4y+4 \right)=-16 \\
\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(x-2)}^{2}}-{{(y-2)}^{2}}=-16 & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{(x-2)}^{2}}}{{{4}^{2}}}-\frac{{{(y-2)}^{2}}}{{{4}^{2}}}=-1 \\
\end{array}$

Shifting the origin at $\left( 2,2 \right)$, we obtain $\frac{{{\text{X}}^{2}}}{{{4}^{2}}}-\frac{{{\text{Y}}^{2}}}{{{4}^{2}}}=-1$, where $\text{x}=\text{X}+2,\text{y}=\text{Y}+2$

This is rectangular hyperbola, whose eccentricity is always $\sqrt{2}$

Illustration 3 

Find the center, eccentricity, foci, directrices and the lengths of the transverse and conjugate axes of the hyperbola, whose equation is ${{(x-1)}^{2}}-2{{(y-2)}^{2}}+6=0$ 

Solution: 

The equation of the hyperbola can be written as ${{(x-1)}^{2}}-2{{(y-2)}^{2}}+6=0$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\frac{{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}}{\sqrt{(6}{{)}^{2}}}+\frac{{{(\text{y}-2)}^{2}}}{{{(\sqrt{3})}^{2}}}=1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\text{Y}}^{2}}}{{{(\sqrt{3})}^{2}}}-\frac{{{\text{X}}^{2}}}{{{(\sqrt{6})}^{2}}}=1$

Where $\text{Y}=\left( \text{y}-2 \right)$ and $\text{X}=\left( \text{x}-1 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ center $:\text{X}=0,\text{Y}=0$ i.e., $\left( \text{x}-1 \right)=0,\text{x}=1\And \left( \text{y}-2 \right)=0,\text{y}=2$.

So, $a=\sqrt{3}$ and $b=\sqrt{6}$ so transverse axis $=2\sqrt{3}$, and conjugate axis $=2\sqrt{6}$.

Also ${{b}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6=3\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)$ i.e., $e=\sqrt{3}$

In $\left( \text{X},\text{Y} \right)$ coordinates, foci are $\left( 0,\pm \text{ae} \right)$

i.e., $\left( 0,\pm 3 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ foci are $\left( 1,2\pm 3 \right)$ i.e., $\left( 1,5 \right)$ and $\left( 1,-1 \right)$

Equations of directrices, $Y=\pm $ a/e

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ directrices $\text{y}-2=\pm \sqrt{3}/\sqrt{3}=\pm 1$ or $\text{y}=3,\text{y}=1$.

Illustration 4 

Find the equation of the hyperbola whose foci are $\left( 8,3 \right)\left( 0,3 \right)$ and eccentricity $=\frac{4}{3}$. 

Solution: 

The center of the hyperbola is the mid-point of the line joining the two foci. So the coordinates of the center are $\left( \frac{8+0}{2},\frac{3+3}{2} \right)$ i.e. $\left( 4,3 \right)$.

Let $2\text{a}$ and $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}$ be the length of transverse and conjugate axes and let e be the eccentricity. Then the equation of the hyperbola is $\frac{{{(\text{x}-4)}^{2}}}{{{\text{a}}^{2}}}-\frac{{{(\text{y}-3)}^{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}^{2}}}=1$ …(i)

Now, distance between the two foci $=2\text{ae}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\sqrt{{{(8-0)}^{2}}+{{(3-3)}^{2}}}=2\text{ae }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ae}=4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a}=3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \because \text{e}=\frac{4}{3} \right)$

Now, ${{b}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}\left( {{e}^{2}}-1 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{b}^{2}}=9\left( -1+\frac{16}{9} \right)=7$

Thus, the equation of the hyperbola is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
{} & \frac{{{(\text{x}-4)}^{2}}}{9}-\frac{{{(\text{y}-3)}^{2}}}{7}=1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\![\!\!\text{ Putting }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ values }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!]\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\
{} & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }7{{\text{x}}^{2}}-9{{\text{y}}^{2}}-56\text{x}+54\text{y}-32=0 \\
\end{array}$

concepts.title: Parametric equation of hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Parametric Equations of the Hyperbola 

Since coordinate $x=a\text{sec}\theta $ and $y=b\text{tan}\theta $ satisfy the equation $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ for all real values of $\theta $ therefore, $x=a\text{sec}\theta ,y=b\text{tan}\theta $ are the parametric equations of the hyperbola $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1,$where the parameter $0\le \theta <2\pi $

Hence, the coordinates of any point on the hyperbola $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ may be taken as ( $a\text{sec}\theta ,b$ $\text{tan}\theta )$. This point is also called the point $\theta $. The angle $\theta $ is called the eccentric angle of the point $\left( \text{asec}\theta ,\text{btan}\theta \right)$ on the hyperbola.3

concepts.title: Conjugate Hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Conjugate Hyperbola  

The hyperbola whose transverse and conjugate axes are respectively the conjugate and transverse axes of a given hyperbola is called the conjugate hyperbola of the given hyperbola.

The conjugate hyperbola of the hyperbola

$\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=-1.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

concepts.title: Properties of Hyperbola and its Conjugate
Equation of a diameter of a hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of a diameter of a hyperbola 

The equation of the diameter bisecting chords of slope of the hyperbola $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ is $y=\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}m}$
